speaker: interviewee
text: German, German death, German Jews who died in World War I. And my uncle's brother is listed in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an ongoing debate that will, will go on as, as long as you and I are alive about the ability to, to reconstruct a nation and rehabilitate a nation. And some people argue today that, that it's, that Germany is still very much under the shadow and in the grip of fascism. What made you have a sense that Germany could, in fact, be rehabilitated and be part of the, the threshold the, the Commonwealth of Nations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the people who I worked with and talked to-- now, realizing that I was in a very small town with a population of about 10,000-- the mayor was, was-- hid out in a monastery, the chief of police also hidden in the same monastery. And these people were very much concerned with, with what was going on. I mean, it wasn't just that they were putting on a show for me. Because after a while, I became like an old shoe. I was around all the time. And, and, you know, they would say things without my interfering. And they were very much concerned with what was happening to the young people of Germany, what the future for Germany was held. And, and they were very much concerned with those DPs out there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so-- and, and I remember listening to the discussions of what, what's gonna happen in the future of Germany? Do you think we'll ever be accepted? Do you think they'll ever forgive us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the people who were there were not Nazis-- I mean, involved in the death camps. They probably, some of them probably were, were party members. But they certainly were low level. Realizing that I was in this little, dinky town, I don't know what it was-- what was going on in, in Stuttgart, which was the next big city, Nurnberg, or even Bonn, which was the capital. I don't know what was going on there. But at, at my level, they were concerned. And I thought, well, this is a grassroots level. And maybe things will change. And I know what's going on in Germany today. They, they still have this tremendous fear of strangers, of the, of the Turks, and of, now of the East Germans, who, by the way, are Germans. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was, there is, there is a lot of, a lot of animosity there are also. The Germans are a very homogeneous unit. They, they accepted me after a while. And I used to joke with them. I said, hey, what are you talking about? I had to leave because-- in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never said I was Jewish. But they must have got the, the fact that I was Jewish. And yeah, but you're German. Now, I don't know whether they said that for my benefit, or they actually believed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the work that you did in the de-nazification, did you come across any of the so-called big names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. This was all low-level stuff. These were common SS troopers. They, they really-- you know, like, it was such a small, dinky town that there really wasn't anything there of, of great consequence. Don't forget. I got overseas. I was 19 years old-- well, 20. And they weren't going to let a kid like me get involved with the big wigs.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were at Spandau. Weren't you at Spandau?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- yeah. But now, I'm, I'm a few years older. And I had gotten a reputation as being a, a, a good linguist. And my boss called me and said to me, we need a linguist in Berlin. I said, OK, fine. You tell me where to go. We'll fly you up there. We'll land in, in a L-19 which is a artillery spotter plane. It's made out of plastic and, and barbed wire-- no, and, and bailing wire. And we-- I flew up to, to Berlin, landed. And I was met by the exec officer of the guard for-- at Spandau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what are we doing? And he said, well, you, sir. And he said, you, you're our linguist. And I said, oh. Where am I-- oh, you're going with me to the jail, to the prison, to Spandau.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, that should be interesting. I thought it was-- you know, it was going to be great. And yes, so I spent a month there. We, we rotated. And when our month was over, I went back to my original unit. And so I saw those people who were still incarcerated-- and this is 1952-- who was still incarcerated at Spandau, including Rudolf Hess, who became the last of the Nazis in that prison. That whole prison was empty except for him. And then he committed suicide not too long ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him? Did you ever talk to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. No-- only-- I was there not for socialized talking, but to, to-- if, if a guard had to say something to one of the prisoners or if they had a medical problem. That's what I was there for. I did not socialize with him, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so no true confessions from former Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, not, not by any means. I watched them. And I did eavesdrop every once in a while. And they, they were talking about, you know, very bland things. The food is rotten. Did you sleep well, that type of thing. So no, there was no true confessions. Don't forget, this is 19-- six years later. So all the true confessions were gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any attempts and-- any attempts by the US military to secrete former Nazis out of the United States--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: To, to South America under the, you know, under-- in the hopes that they might be able to have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Paperclip, you're talking, you're talking about Operation Paperclip. Yeah. That was what they did. I, I-- don't forget. I was in this little town. I was detailed once in a while to go on border patrol along the Czech border. I interrogated Sudetendeutsches. Those that had anything of value were immediately whisked away to King Barracks outside of Frankfurt. The other ones who were bonafide-- what we call bonafides, they were refugees-- went to some DP camp. I think it was Grunewald. Anyway, one of those places. Again, I was at the first echelon. And I, I didn't get to King Barracks where they were doing the interrogations and did sit-in as, as a as a training vehicle to see how they were handling some of the people. But I had no idea who they were talking to. Because they just didn't tell me and watched them do the interrogations. And I, I had my list of what we call EEI, Essential Elements of Information. That's like a checklist of what to ask for. If I followed that, wrote it up, and then that plus the, the subject was sent forward. So I was-- in, in those days, I was very low level individual. I think I was a corporal. Yeah. So corporals don't make major decisions. Colonels do, but not corporals.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I'm interested in, in the process and, and what you learn in the process, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for instance, let's say that it was a, it was a military person, a lieutenant, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an infantry lieutenant. And I would do map tracking with him. Map tracking is I, I had, I had a map of the area. And I'd say, OK. We are now at, at this point, where you crossed over into our zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did you get here? Well, I followed this road. And I would take him back along the road. What did you see here? What's on this hill? Is there an observation-- and some of the things I already knew, you know from-- I'd say, what's on this hill?

speaker: interviewee
text: And if he, if he told me nothing, I'd say, wait a minute. You-- either you didn't look, or you're lying. There's an observation point up there. Oh, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's over here? Let's go back further along this road. What's over here in this area? Nothing. Oh, there's a garrison there. Who's in that garrison. Well, we've got the 118th Armored Cav unit or whatever. And then, then, we were getting on the right track. So I, I included what I knew to try to trick him into, you know, realizing-- thinking that I already knew more than I really did. And by map tracking him all the way back to his garrison and finding out what was along the route, route that he took and have him identify tank parks, artillery pieces, units, POL dumps-- petroleum, oil, and lubrication dumps-- ammo dumps, that type of thing, would elicit information from him. And if he cooperated, that was fine. If he didn't cooperate, every once in awhile, we'd ship 'em back. Oh, so nonetheless, it gave you not only an insight into essentially the, the organization and the structure of the German military, but the psychology of the German military.

speaker: interviewee
text: Czech military.

speaker: interviewer
text: Czech military.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We didn't, we didn't-- there was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of Sudetenland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were-- Sudeten-- these were Sudeten, which was Czechoslovakia. So I worked with the constabularies, the 16th Regiment, or 16th Squadron, out of [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was part of the counterintelligence people and worked out of [NON-ENGLISH], which is right across from the other side. And so I got to be quite fluent and conversant in the, what we call the order of battle of what was on the other side, what was the opposing forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to start bringing this to conclusion. One last question in this-- do you remember the people in, in the DP camps, any, any strong--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [SIGHS] Total sadness-- what these people went through is unbearable, unbelievable. They were slave laborers. They were beaten. They were mistreated. They were abused. They were starved. They went through a living hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first time I went there, it broke me up. I, I had to walk out of there. And my boss said, what's going on? And I said, well, this is, this is just traumatic. And this is six years later. And he said-- or five years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, it's your job. You get in there. And you make sure that these people are taken care of. And so I did whatever I had to do to make sure they had blankets, and clothing, and food, and medical, and that they were taken care of. And eventually, they were-- a lot of them went to Australia. So we processed them out of there to Australia. And-- but, it was, it was-- for me as a kid, it was traumatic, traumatic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What broke you up? Was it the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Seeing the conditions. They were still-- they were still gone, looking at-- you know. And they knew I was an American, because I was in an American uniform. But the stories that they told was just-- and you know, in German-- and what they went through in the slave labor camps. I mean, it was just, just horrendous.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've talked a little bit about your military work in the 1950s. Just if you can in, in a very brief way, sort of fill in the blanks for us. You went to school. You graduated from-- you went back-- after, after Germany, then you went to college. I'm trying, I'm trying to remember the sequence here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I, I was discharged from the regular army in, in 1954. And in September, started college. Now, I had taken some courses while I was on active duty called USAFI, which came out of Madison, Wisconsin. And so I had already about a-- one semester in. Went to college, finished, went to summer school. And I was gonna go back in the regular army. And then in September of 1956, I met Belle Anne, my wife. And we got married in '57. And I just-- I, I did go to work as a carpenter's helper. And then that folded. And then Belle Anne more or less convinced me I should become a schoolteacher and went back to school to get my certification, graduated, finished up, and went to-- got a job with the Oak Creek school system south of Milwaukee, and lasted with them a year and a half, and then went to Milwaukee, where I stayed for 30 years. But I will go on active duty. I was commissioned a lieutenant in 1960 and then immediately was put on duty with the 5th Army Intelligence School. I'm a military intelligence officer. And that was my branch, even as an enlisted person [BACKGROUND NOISE] and was-- went every summer to teach at the 5th Army Intel School, and went from, from student to instructor to department director to director of instruction, which is like the curriculum coordinator. And I also was in a unit, in a tactical intelligence unit-- that means going out with the infantry-- a strategic intelligence unit, where we did strategic studies. And then I went to work for the Intelligence and Security Command, which was a combination of the cryptological service as well as counterintelligence, and stayed with them for a grand total of 16 years, and ended up as their chief of staff for mobilization. And that's when I ended up as a full colonel. And it was a glorious career. I loved every minute of it. Fantastic human beings-- I worked with people who were giants. In fact, one of them, Sidney Thomas Weinstein was-- I knew him as a full colonel. Then he got promoted. He ended up a three-star general.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I once asked him-- this was in private. We were on a first-name basis outside. The colonel-- I was a lieutenant colonel doesn't talk to general by first name basis. Anyway, I said, Tom, what made you decide to become an army officer? And he said, what else does a Jewish-- poor Jewish kid from central New Jersey do? And he said, what made you decide to do this? And I said, well, I, I owe this country a tremendous, tremendous debt. They, they provided me with a home, with an education. They allowed us-- my sister, my mother, grandmother-- to come here. And I owe them everything. So, I mean, it's, it's-- plus, I loved it. I mean, it wasn't, it wasn't just one thing. It was many things. And in Schwarzkopf's book, he asked Tom the same question. And he, and he got the same answer. So I roared when I saw that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But those are the kind of giants, absolute giants. Those are the kind of people that I really enjoyed working with. I mean, they were just super human beings. And I'm grateful to this country. They educated me. If-- I'm sorry you can't-- on this video, you can't see this house. I mean, we've, we've, we've been so, so fortunate. I'm the luckiest guy in the world-- fantastic wife, fantastic children. I've had a fantastic career, I, I worked for Milwaukee public schools for 30 years. I retired from there and went to work for the Whitnall School District as a guidance counselor. And at the age of 60, they allowed me to, to work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I've been so fortunate and so lucky. So I thank God every day for the privilege and the honor of having been able to, to serve this country and to have done something for the, the, the children of Milwaukee. I worked in an alternative high school for 10 years. Now, most people don't understand what that means.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we took the children who were, who were assaulters, who carried weapons to school. And I worked there for 10 years with outstanding human beings. My closest friend, Bill [INAUDIBLE], who-- when we had fights-- he was the guy who backed me up. I would break up fights. And he'd back me up. And Dolores Grunke was-- [? assisted ?] [? guy-- ?] was-- I assisted her as a guidance counselor. And we had-- we, we really ran a good school. And it's still in existence. But we're both retired. And now I'm an auxiliary police officer in the city-- village-- of Whitefish Bay and get a chance to do some shooting, and ride in a squad car, and work with tremendous people, outstanding human beings. And I'm still doing something for the village and, you know, other people. We do make some arrests with, with juveniles. And I do counsel I do get involved with them and try to explain to them that the road they're going down is the road to hell. It's not going to, it's not going to help them. And the only way they are going to succeed in this world is by flying, flying right. Doing something for themselves and get away from these gangs and get away from the drugs and take care of your problem. Don't let other people dictate to you what has to be done. And that's, that's, that's my creed. When we landed in 1951 in, in Bremerhaven, they took us off the-- we got off the ship. And a very famous gentleman said to all of us, soldiers, when you leave here, I want you-- well, I want you to behave in such a manner that when you leave here, people are going to say, gee, sorry to see 'em go. And that was Dwight David Eisenhower. And I've tried to live by that. And I'll always try to do the best job I possibly can so that when people do-- when I do leave, people will, will, say, gee. We're sorry to see you go. And that's really-- that's, that's been the case in, in, in Milwaukee and in, in Whitnall, and even the army. I'll have to show you upstairs a beautiful picture of the, of the headquarters, of the Intelligence and Security Command and what the people who I worked with wrote on that. They were sorry to see me go. And one of the saddest days of my life was when I had to retire. But I knew it was inevitable. [LAUGHS] They don't let you stay too long. I mean, I put in-- I was 51 days shy of 40 years in the military.

speaker: interviewer
text: One is reminded-- you know, in closing-- one's reminded of all the, the horror that was done by, by the German army. And, and listening to you and, and essentially all of the good that your military training, you feel, has, has given you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's just, it's, it's an interesting contrast.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, see, not all Germans were bad. A lot of them tried to do the best they could. My mother's friend, [? Luda ?] [? Schlumberger, ?] the man who hid us out, they tried to help. And there's-- there were-- not-- the whole country wasn't evil. It was run by evil people. But that doesn't mean that every person living in Germany was evil. And a lot of them, to this day, rue what they did, regret terribly some of the things they did. Because I talked to some of these people. And they said, oh, what a mistake we made. Now you're going to-- and I told them, now you're paying for it. And you will pay and pay and pay for the rest of your life. But that doesn't mean that every Jew is going to hate you. There are some who say, hey, I don't know what you did. And I, I, I can't-- I cannot, deep within, within me, hate anyone. That's, that's doesn't do any, anyone any good. And I know there a lot of Jews who hate the Germans. The mere fact that they speak German is enough to turn them off. But I say this was a period of total national madness. And sometimes, we have to say, hey, enough is enough. Let's not reach down to that level. And never forget. I don't want anyone to ever forget what happened. We don't want this to be repeated. But to go through life hating them, I just can't-- I, I can't bring myself to do it. I really can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that we could go on talking for, for a long time. But in, in terms of, in terms of this, is there's something I haven't asked you that you think is important to say in this sort of brief video of your experiences surrounding the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I, I, I I'm just glad that, that this film is being, is being made. Because I'm not going to be around forever. And the film will probably be around for many, many years after I'm gone. I'm delighted to, to do this. It gives me a chance to honor my mother for what she did-- heroically had enough foresight to get us out of there. Because if, if, if it would have been up to the rest of the family, we would have succumbed. She was the one who had the courage, the gumption, the foresight to get us out. And she had a, a very, very difficult life. I, I honor my mother from the-- till the day she died. And as she said to our son, we never exchanged a cross word simply because I knew what she went through. I saw it with my eyes. I saw the, the, the garment industry where she worked. And I saw the, the filthy toilets and the boxes packed up against the, the fire escape so that she-- in case of fire, she would have succumbed-- what she went through all those years. And I honor her memory by doing this film, even though she could never could do it. I'm doing it also for her sake. And I also would like to, to make mention of my wife, who has been so tremendously supportive of what I saw as my obligation and duty, not just to, to the US army. And we were joking before about when I retired, I lost my mistress. But she, she was so supportive and did such a tremendous job in raising our four kids and always was there for me as, as my mentor, to give me advice as to how-- which way to go. She was my consoler. She did a tremendous amount of, of, of, uplifting things for me and guided me in, in, in many aspects, which she probably doesn't even realize the things that she said to me that, that played a tremendous impact on me. And today is our 39th wedding anniversary. And I just would like to honor her also.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, introductions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello. I'm Belle Anne Freund, married to Phil. And together, we have this wonderful family. Our oldest is Peggy, Margarette Lynn. The second, our first son, is Mark Joseph, married to Laura. Second son is Perry Michael, married to Sue. Peggy is married to Steve. Unfortunately, he wasn't able to be here today. Our youngest is Jackie, Jacqueline Miriam, married to Craig. And we have Joseph and Samuel, belonging to-- to Craig and Jackie. We have Christopher and Rachel, belonging to Peggy. And Matthew belongs to Perry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And your mom. [LAUGHTER] What else? We're very fortunate to be here today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark wanted to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just some of the things I've, I've noticed about my-- both my father and my mother, some of the things that have affected my father's life after surviving the Holocaust, I think he has devoted his life to-- I would call public servitude. He's been a teacher. And he was in the military. And he's, he's in many volunteer organizations. He hasn't ever gone into private business, where the sole purpose was just profit for a corporation. He's-- I think he did that because he feels he owes-- his life was saved. And he owes-- he has to make more out of his life. And by going into the public servitude, he's, he's repaying his survival. And he's, he's giving back his, his life that was saved. Another thing that, that I learned as I was growing up was-- from my father and from my grandmother-- that you can't not have an education. You have to go to college. You have to get a degree.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything can happen to you. You can't rely on having family wealth. You have to be able to make it on your own. And I think my-- that influenced my parents to push all of our-- all of my brothers and sisters and me to go in-- go on and get further education and to create a career [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and to, to make the most out of our life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All of us are-- us children have become very hard workers, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where we're leading exemplary lives, I feel. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] What else? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I lived with my grandmother for two and 1/2 or three years when I was in college. So I got to know her pretty well. She never voiced any animosity towards the German-- the Germany that existed after the war. She felt very free to go back to Germany and travel around. She still had a friend, Mrs. [? Schlumberger, ?] who helped her in the war. I believe after Kristallnacht, she took the kids to, to Mrs. [? Schlumberger's ?] estate in the country to get them out of Munich. And she viewed Mrs. [? Schlumberger ?] as her base in Munich. So she, she would travel occasionally to visit her until Grandma died. When grandma was in her late 70s, she was still haunted by some of the events that happened to her in Munich. She was-- she would have bad dreams. And she would wake up at night. She would remember her sister. She never talked about what happened to her husband. It was very traumatic for her. She, she was very happy about all of her grandchildren and the prospect of them getting married. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what else to, to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine. Anyone else want to say anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, did you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to go first. Well, I remember when Rachel was born. And my grandma came to the hospital and saw her. She was concerned about the name I gave her, because it was too Jewish. And she didn't want me to name Rachel Rachel. And to me, it's important that my children learn about their heritage and about their background so that they never forget where they came from and be grateful for all that they have here and all that they can accomplish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess growing up, my parents instilled a very high moral standard in us and a very high work ethic. Because anything could be taken away from you at any time. That's probably why they always instilled education and to just-- any education is good. Because obviously, material things don't necessarily last. But what your education is, nobody can ever take that away. And I'm very glad my dad made it. I wish his dad made it. It would have been nice to meet my grandfather. And that's why I named my older son after him. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what you see here is the Freund clan. And if it wouldn't have been for [? Tia ?] Freund Lauchheimer-- she was born Lauchheimer, became a Freund then became a Herz-- if it wouldn't have been for her courage and her foresight in seeing what was going on, this never would have occurred. We're very fortunate that we have such a fine family and such good, solid children. And we certainly hope that their, that their children will also follow in the same footsteps of their parents and of their grandparents and of their great-grandparents. It has been, it has been a sincere honor and privilege to be able to make this film, not just for us, but for posterity and for-- to make sure that the world never forgets. So I thank you. That's it.
